title: Advanced concessive and contrast structures
lesson: Formal English offers a rich set of concessive structures that go well beyond 'although'. 'Much as I admire her' fronts the verb of feeling for emphasis ('Much as + subject + verb'). 'However much/hard + subject + verb' concedes that no degree of effort changes the outcome. 'For all + noun phrase' means 'in spite of' ('For all his talent, he failed'). 'Granted (that) + clause' concedes a point before countering it. 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, typically an adjective or adverbial, not a full finite clause. 'Notwithstanding' functions as a preposition ('notwithstanding the objections') or, more formally, follows its noun. These patterns differ chiefly in what must follow them, which is what tells the right connector apart from its near-synonyms.

items.text: {1} I admire her courage, I simply cannot support such a reckless decision.

blanks.hint: fronts a verb of feeling: ___ + subject + verb

items.explanation: 'Much as I admire her courage' is the fixed concessive pattern 'Much as + subject + verb' meaning 'although I greatly admire'. 'As much' inverts the words and is ungrammatical here; 'Despite' must be followed by a noun or -ing, not a finite clause; 'However' would need an adjective/adverb ('However much').

items.text: {1} hard she tried, she could never quite master the pronunciation.

blanks.hint: ___ + adverb + subject + verb = no degree of effort helped

items.explanation: 'However + adverb (hard) + subject + verb' concedes that no degree of trying changed the result. 'Much as' takes a verb directly, not an adverb; 'Whatever' takes a noun/clause, not the adverb 'hard'; 'Albeit' cannot introduce a finite clause like 'she tried'.

items.text: {1} his undeniable talent, he never achieved the recognition he deserved.
items.explanation: 'For all + noun phrase' means 'in spite of' / 'despite': 'For all his talent' = 'despite his talent'. 'Much as' and 'However' require a following clause/adjective, not a bare noun phrase; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase that qualifies something already stated, not a fronted 'in spite of' phrase.

items.text: {1} that the proposal has merit, we still lack the funds to implement it this year.

blanks.hint: concede a point before countering: ___ (that) + clause

items.explanation: 'Granted (that) + clause' concedes a point you are about to argue against: 'Granted that the proposal has merit, we still...'. 'Albeit' cannot take a 'that'-clause; 'Despite' takes a noun phrase or -ing, never a finite 'that'-clause; 'Much as' is followed directly by subject + verb ('Much as the proposal has merit'), not by 'that'.

items.text: The committee approved the revised budget, {1} with considerable reluctance.

blanks.hint: introduces a concessive phrase (no finite verb), here 'with reluctance'

items.explanation: 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase qualifying what precedes it: 'approved..., albeit with considerable reluctance'. The other options all require a following clause or different structure ('much as'/'granted' need a subject and verb; 'however' would need an adjective/adverb), so none fits a bare prepositional phrase.

items.text: {1} the strenuous objections of the board, the merger went ahead as planned.

blanks.hint: a formal preposition: ___ + noun phrase = in spite of

items.explanation: 'Notwithstanding' is a formal preposition meaning 'in spite of', so it is followed directly by the noun phrase 'the strenuous objections'. 'Much as' requires a subject + verb ('Much as the board objected'); 'However' needs a following adjective/adverb; 'Even though' introduces a finite clause, not a bare noun phrase.

items.text: Hard {1} he tried, he never managed to win her approval.
items.explanation: The fronted concessive pattern 'Adjective/Adverb + though + subject + verb' ('Hard though he tried') is an emphatic equivalent of 'although he tried hard'. 'Despite' takes a noun/-ing, not a clause; 'however' would itself stand at the front ('However hard he tried'), not after the fronted word; 'much' does not link a fronted adverb to a clause in this slot.

items.text: Exhausted {1} she was, she insisted on finishing the marathon.

blanks.hint: fronted adjective + ___ + subject + be = concessive 'as ... as'

items.explanation: 'Adjective + as + subject + be' ('Exhausted as she was') is a concessive inversion meaning 'although she was exhausted', common in formal and literary English. 'Though as' doubles the connector and is ungrammatical; 'however' would precede the adjective ('However exhausted she was'); 'despite' cannot govern the finite clause 'she was'.

items.text: The plan is undeniably bold. {1}, I remain convinced it can be made to work.

blanks.hint: a conjunct linking back to the previous sentence: 'in spite of that'

items.explanation: 'Even so' is a sentence conjunct meaning 'in spite of that', linking back to the preceding sentence and standing alone before a comma. 'Even though' must introduce a subordinate clause within the same sentence; 'Much as' needs a following subject + verb; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a free-standing connector before a full main clause.

items.text: {1} the figures may look discouraging, they conceal a genuine improvement in efficiency.

blanks.hint: subordinator used concessively: ___ + finite clause = although

items.explanation: 'While' (or 'Whilst') used concessively introduces a finite clause and means 'although': 'While the figures may look discouraging'. 'For all' and 'Notwithstanding' are followed by a noun phrase, not a finite clause; 'Albeit' cannot head a full finite clause with its own subject and modal ('the figures may look').

items.text: {1} as he might, he could not prise the rusted lock open.

blanks.hint: bare-infinitive verb fronted: '___ as + subject + might/may'

items.explanation: '[Verb] as + subject + might/may' ('Try as he might') is an emphatic concessive meaning 'however hard he tried' or 'although he tried'; the verb stands in the bare infinitive at the front. 'However' needs a following adjective or adverb ('However hard he tried'); 'Much' belongs to the frame 'Much as + subject + finite verb', not the bare 'as he might' pattern; 'Even' does not form this fronted concessive.

items.text: The scheme is costly and the schedule punishing. {1} that as it may, the board is determined to proceed.

blanks.hint: fixed subjunctive idiom: '___ that as it may' = even so

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed formal idiom using the subjunctive 'be', meaning 'even so / nonetheless'; it concedes the previous statement before countering it. None of the alternatives completes this frozen expression: 'However that as it may', 'Much that as it may' and 'Granted that as it may' are all ungrammatical, and 'Granted' would instead be followed by '(that) + clause'.

items.text: The novel is overlong and its plot implausible. {1} said, it contains some of the finest prose she has ever written.

blanks.hint: conjunct '___ said,' concedes the prior point before a contrast

items.explanation: 'That said' is a conjunct — a reduced form of 'that having been said' — meaning 'nevertheless / even so', acknowledging a preceding criticism before offering a counterpoint. The other words cannot precede 'said' in this fixed phrase: 'However' requires a following adjective or adverb, 'Granted' takes a '(that)-'clause, and 'Much' needs the frame 'Much as + subject + verb'.

items.text: {1} the cost, the government insists that the flood defences must be built.

blanks.hint: '___ + noun phrase' concedes every possibility: 'no matter what'

items.explanation: 'Whatever + noun phrase' ('Whatever the cost') is an elliptical concessive meaning 'no matter what the cost may be'. 'However' must be followed by an adjective or adverb ('However great the cost'), not a bare noun phrase; 'Much as' requires a subject and finite verb; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase qualifying something already stated, not a fronted 'no-matter-what' condition.

items.text: The evidence against him was largely circumstantial. The jury, {1}, returned a verdict of guilty.

blanks.hint: parenthetical conjunct between commas = 'in spite of that'

items.explanation: 'Nonetheless' (like 'nevertheless') is a conjunct meaning 'in spite of that / even so'; it can sit parenthetically between commas inside the clause and points back to the previous sentence. 'Even though' and 'much as' must each introduce their own subordinate clause with a subject and verb, which the bare parenthetical slot does not provide; 'albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a free-standing sentence connector.
